summary: Ronacher argues that MCP’s current design is less effective than code generation because it relies too heavily on inference and consumes too much context. He says even domain-specific automation works better when LLMs generate scripts that can be reviewed, validated, and run repeatedly. His blog migration example shows an LLM-to-code-to-LLM pipeline that produced trustworthy results by focusing on verifiable transformations rather than direct inference. He concedes MCP can be useful in some UI-driving scenarios, but notes that scripts outperform tool calls when the domain is known. The conclusion is to favor code-first agentic workflows and only use inference as a judging layer after bulk execution.
key_points: MCP is not truly composable and is context-inefficient compared to CLI or code-based approaches.
Automation at scale favors generated code because it is reviewable, repeatable, and easier to validate than inferred actions.
A multi-step pipeline (generate code, run it, then use LLMs to judge diffs) yields higher trust and lower regression risk.
When tasks are known and structured, scripts outperform MCP tool calls and avoid real-time inference costs.
The future may require better sandboxes and abstractions that combine code generation with selective LLM judging.
money_quotes: MCP suffers from two major flaws: it isn’t truly composable and it demands too much context.
Current MCP will always be harder to use than writing code, primarily due to the reliance on inference.
We need to figure out how to ensure that our automated task actually works correctly at scale.
For that repetition, there's a very strong argument to be made for always using code.
What’s key is that I actually trusted this process at the end because I could review the approach.
Once the script is written, I can execute it 100, 200, or even 300 times without requiring any further inference.
In any case I can only encourage people to bypass MCP and to explore what else is possible.
key_insight: LLMs are most powerful when they generate verifiable code and reserve inference for judging results, not driving every step.
